radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:   CT CHEST W/O CONTRAST Q411

INDICATION:  ___ year old man with iatrogenic PTX. Please obtain CT while on-40
suction.// Extent of PTX.

TECHNIQUE:  Multidetector CT images of the chest were obtained without
intravenous contrast.  Sagittal and coronal reformations were also performed.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Spiral Acquisition 2.7 s, 42.7 cm; CTDIvol = 10.9 mGy (Body) DLP = 465.5
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 466 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  Prior study from ___.

FINDINGS: 

Heart is normal in size.  Coronary artery calcification is mild.  Aorta is
normal in caliber.  Pulmonary arteries are also normal in caliber.

There is no lymphadenopathy.  There are no pleural or pericardial effusions.

There is a trace right-sided pneumothorax. A pigtail catheter terminates along
the anteromedial right mid chest.

Emphysema is moderate to severe.

Right basilar opacities suggest minor atelectasis, but somewhat decreased.
Very small 3 mm nodule in the right lower lobe (302:135) appears unchanged. 
Additional small nodule measures 4 mm (302:135), difficult to compare to the
recent prior study since there were more extensive opacities before.

Phase of respiration is expiratory.  Each mainstem bronchus, particularly the
right, is almost fully collapsed suggesting malacia.

At the base of the right chest, a pleural based mass measuring up to 30 mm in
diameter appears unchanged.

Simple cyst again demonstrated in the left lateral segments of the liver.

There are no suspicious bone lesions.  Bones appear demineralized.

IMPRESSION: 

1.  Trace right-sided pneumothorax with chest tube in situ.  No short-term
change in pleural mass.

2.  Moderate to severe emphysema.

3.  Finding suggest bronchomalacia.

4.  Two small nodules in the right lower lobe amenable for follow-up
surveillance depending on clinical circumstances and result of pleural mass
biopsy.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CTA CHEST WITH CONTRAST

INDICATION:  ___ year old man with tachycardia and hypoxia, as well as possible
malignancy.  // Please evaluate for pulmonary embolism.

TECHNIQUE:  Axial multidetector CT images were obtained through the thorax
after the uneventful administration of intravenous contrast.  Reformatted
coronal, sagittal, thin slice axial images, and oblique maximal intensity
projection images were submitted to PACS and reviewed.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Spiral Acquisition 2.8 s, 37.2 cm; CTDIvol = 10.1 mGy (Body) DLP = 373.4
mGy-cm.
   2) Stationary Acquisition 0.6 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 3.3 mGy (Body) DLP = 1.7
mGy-cm.
   3) Stationary Acquisition 3.6 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 19.9 mGy (Body) DLP =
9.9 mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 385 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  CT chest ___, CT abdomen pelvis ___

FINDINGS: 

BASE OF NECK: The visualized base of the neck is unremarkable.

THORACIC LYMPH NODES: Supraclavicular, axillary, mediastinal, and hilar lymph
nodes are not pathologically enlarged .

CARDIO-MEDIASTINUM: The thoracic aortic is normal in caliber.  The heart is
not enlarged. There is no pericardial effusion.  There is mild to moderate
coronary artery calcification.  The pulmonary arteries are patent to the
subsegmental level without evidence of pulmonary embolus.

AIRWAY: The airways are patent to the segmental level.

LUNGS: There is moderate to severe emphysema. Three right lower lobe nodules
measuring 1-2 mm are unchanged (301:124, 135).

PLEURA: There is no pleural effusion.  A 2.6 x 2.4 cm pleurally based mass of
the right lung base appears unchanged from the prior study of ___ but is new from ___ lesion is status post biopsy (2:88).

CHEST CAGE: No worrisome lytic or sclerotic lesion is identified.

UPPER ABDOMEN: This study is not tailored for evaluation of the abdomen.
Allowing for this, a 1.5 cm left hepatic lobe hypodensity is noted, not
significantly changed from ___.

IMPRESSION:


1. No evidence of pulmonary embolism or an acute aortic abnormality.
2. Redemonstrated 2.6 cm pleurally based mass at the right lung base, status
post biopsy consistent with chronic inflammation.
3. Multiple 1-2 mm right lower lobe pulmonary nodules are unchanged.  The need
for followup imaging depends on staging and management considerations
regarding the patient's extrathoracic malignancy.  Otherwise ___
guidelines for management of incidentally discovered pulmonary nodules would
apply.  See recommendations below.

RECOMMENDATION(S):  See impression above.

For incidentally detected multiple solid pulmonary nodules smaller than 6mm,
no CT follow-up is recommended in a low-risk patient, and an optional CT
follow-up in 12 months is recommended in a high-risk patient.

See the ___ ___ Society Guidelines for the Management of Pulmonary
Nodules Incidentally Detected on CT" for comments and reference:
___

radiology_facts.report_text:
HISTORY:  ___ female with ambulatory desat.  Please assess for PE.

COMPARISON:  No prior CT chest for comparison. Only one chest radiography
___.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT of the chest was obtained after administration of intravenous
contrast.  Images were reformatted in the axial, coronal, sagittal and oblique
planes.

FINDINGS:  No filling defect in pulmonary arteries that could suggest evidence
of pulmonary embolism. Diffuse ground-glass opacity in both lungs.  In the
right upper lobe, there are 5.6-mm and 3.3-mm lung nodules (2:15, 36).
Enlarged bilateral hilar lymph nodes (more predominantly on the right side,
measuring up to 12 mm) and mediastinal lymph nodes are present. Septal
thickening can be identified, most marked in the lung bases. Heart size is
within normal limits.  There is no pericardial or pleural effusion.  Aorta is
unremarkable and normal in size.  Limited evaluation of upper abdominal organs
is unremarkable.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Negative examination for pulmonary embolism.
2.  Diffuse ground-glass opacity associated with septal thickening in bases
and scattered lung nodules in right upper lobe.  Asymmetrical hilar (right >
left) and mediastinal enlarged lymph nodes.  Differential diagnosis of lung
findings includes viral or PCP pneumonia, drug reaction, acute eosinophilic
pneumonia and pulmonary edema. It is uncertain whether the lymph nodes are
related to the acute process or if they could be due to a separate entity such
as sarcoid and less likely lymphoma. With this in mind, follow up CT in 6 to 8
weeks after treatment for the acute pulmonary process is recommended to ensure
improvement of the lymph nodes.

radiology_facts.report_text:
INDICATION:  ___ female with pulmonary nodules and DIP.

COMPARISON:  ___.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT axial images through the chest were obtained following the
administration of intravenous contrast and displayed at 5- and 1.25-mm
collimation.  A series of sagittal and coronal images were reformatted for
review.

FINDINGS:  Widespread ground glass opacity has largely resolved.  A few
scattered ground glass opacities remain.  Pulmonary nodules include four right
upper lobe nodules measuring up to 5 mm (4:69, 100, 101, 110) and a 4-mm
subpleural right lower lobe nodule.  Scattered mediastinal and hilar lymph
nodes including a 13-mm right hilar lymph node have markedly decreased in
extent.  There are no pleural or pericardial effusions. The central airways
are patent.

While not tailored for subdiaphragmatic evaluation, the visualized adrenal
glands are unremarkable.

No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Near-complete resolution of diffuse ground glass opacity consistent with
resolving DIP.

2.  Stable noncalcified pulmonary nodules.  Followup chest CT in one year is
recommended to ensure stability.

radiology_facts.report_text:
HISTORY:  Smoking, with a strong family history of lung cancer.  Evaluate
pulmonary nodules.

COMPARISON:  ___.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT of the chest was obtained without intravenous contrast, with
axial images displayed as 1 mm and 5-mm collimation.  Coronal and sagittal
reformations were also obtained.

FINDINGS:  The heart is normal in size.  The great vessels are unremarkable.
No pathologically enlarged mediastinal, hilar, or axillary lymph nodes by CT
size criteria are identified.

Multiple pulmonary nodules are unchanged from the prior study, including
nodules that measure up to 5 mm in the right upper lobe (5:69, 100, 107, and
115), and a subpleural right lower lobe nodule.  However, there are new small
ground-glass opacities bilaterally, most severe in the left upper lobe.  These
findings are concerning for DIP, which is of new onset. There is no evidence
of emphysema.  The airways are patent to the subsegmental level. There is no
pleural effusion.

Limited views of the subdiaphragmatic regions revealed normal-appearing
adrenal glands.  

The osseous structures are unremarkable.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Stable pulmonary nodules.
2.  New ground glass opacities bilaterally, most severe in the left upper
lobe.  Findings suggest DIP given provided history of smoking.  Eosinophilic
pneumonia is considered less likely.  A follow-up Chest CT in 6 months is
suggested.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CT CHEST W/O CONTRAST

INDICATION:  ___ year old woman with extensive smoking history, ground glass
opacities bilaterally on ___ CT, most severe in the left upper lobe.  //
Assess for progression of GGO's, and nodules since ___

TECHNIQUE:  Multidetector helical scanning of the chest was performed without
intravenous contrast agent reconstructed as contiguous 5- and 1.25-mm thick
axial, 2.5-mm thick coronal and parasagittal, and 8 x 8 mm MIPs axial images

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Spiral Acquisition 6.0 s, 38.9 cm; CTDIvol = 5.4 mGy (Body) DLP = 208.1
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 208 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  ___ CT chest without contrast

FINDINGS: 

Axillary, mediastinal, and hilar lymph nodes are not pathologically enlarged. 
Heart size is normal.  There is no pericardial effusion.  No appreciable
coronary artery calcifications.  Thoracic aorta and main pulmonary arteries
are normal in caliber.

Partially imaged upper abdomen is unremarkable.  A 5 mm right upper lobe
subpleural nodule is unchanged (5:64).  A 5 mm right middle lobe nodule is
unchanged (5:150).  A 4 mm subpleural right lower lobe nodule is unchanged
(05:105).  Multiple additional sub 5 mm nodules, particularly in the right
upper lobe are also unchanged.  No new or growing pulmonary nodules
identified.  Previously noted areas of ground-glass opacification with upper
lobe predominance have nearly resolved.  There is no consolidation or pleural
effusion.

The skeletal structures of the thorax do not show suspicious lytic or
sclerotic lesions.

Partially imaged upper abdomen is unremarkable.

IMPRESSION: 

1.  Multiple pulmonary nodules measuring 5 mm or less are stable dating back
to ___.  No further follow-up is indicated.

2.  Previously seen scattered ground-glass opacities in the upper lobes have
nearly resolved.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CT CHEST W/CONTRAST

INDICATION:  ___ year old smoker with history of asthma, +family history of
lung cancer, treated for the last 4 weeks for pneumonia without resolution  //
Pneumonia? Mass?

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT axial images were acquired through the chestfollowing the
uneventful administration of intravenous contrast. Coronal, sagittal, and MIP
reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Spiral Acquisition 5.9 s, 38.4 cm; CTDIvol = 8.2 mGy (Body) DLP = 308.4
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 308 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  Chest CT from ___

FINDINGS: 

NECK, THORACIC INLET, AXILLAE, CHEST WALL: The thyroid gland is not
visualized.  There is no axillary adenopathy.

UPPER ABDOMEN: This study is not tailored for subdiaphragmatic evaluation. 
Visualized upper abdominal structures are unremarkable.  Incidental note is
made of an accessory spleen.

MEDIASTINUM: Mediastinal lymph nodes are not enlarged by CT size criteria,
measuring up to 7 mm in short axis in the precarinal station and 9 mm in the
subcarinal station.  There is no mediastinal mass or hematoma.

HILA: There is mild, confluent bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy.

HEART and PERICARDIUM: Heart size is normal.  No pericardial effusion.
PLEURA: No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.
LUNG:

1. PARENCHYMA:  There is diffuse ground-glass opacification throughout
bilateral lungs in a peribronchovascular distribution.  There is associated
septal thickening.  Pulmonary nodules are stable dating back to ___,
including a 6 mm right middle lobe nodule (5:159), multiple right upper lobe
nodules measuring up to 5 mm (5: 80, 108, 111, 122), a 4 mm right lower lobe
nodule (5:110), and a 3 mm left lower lobe nodule (5:64).
2. AIRWAYS:  The airways are patent to the subsegmental level bilaterally. 
There is mild bronchial wall thickening, most pronounced in the bilateral
upper lobes.
3. VESSELS:  The thoracic aorta, as well as the main, left and right pulmonary
arteries are normal in caliber.

CHEST CAGE: No acute fracture or aggressive osseous lesion.

IMPRESSION:


1. Diffuse ground-glass opacification throughout bilateral lungs in a
peribronchovascular distribution.  Differential considerations include
atypical/viral infection, sarcoidosis, hypersensitivity pneumonitis, drug
toxicity, or respiratory bronchiolitis interstitial lung disease given the
history smoking.
2. Bilateral pulmonary nodules measuring up to 6 mm are stable dating back to
___.
3. Mild confluent bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy.

radiology_facts.report_text:
HISTORY:  ___ female with ovarian cancer, restaging examination.

TECHNIQUE:  CT of the abdomen was performed post administration of oral
contrast and without intravenous contrast followed by CT of the chest, abdomen
and pelvis post administration of intravenous contrast, additional delayed
imaging was performed through the abdomen.

COMPARISON:  CT of ___.

FINDINGS:

CT CHEST POST ADMINISTRATION OF INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST:

There is minimal biapical fibrosis.  There is a 2-mm ill-defined pulmonary
opacity in the right apex (image 11, series 3).  Similarly, there is a 2-mm
pleural-based opacity in the lingula (image 40, series 3).  There is minimal
subpleural fibrosis in the left lung anteriorly, most likely representing
prior radiation treatment.

There is no significant intrathoracic or axillary lymphadenopathy.  There is
no pericardial or pleural effusion.

There is no aortic dissection or pulmonary embolism.  Coronary artery
atherosclerosis is present.

CT ABDOMEN WITH AND WITHOUT INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST:

There is a large heterogenous ___ x 84 mm mass with its epicenter at the
splenic hilum, this mass has a cystic component measuring 86 x 57 mm invading
the spleen.  In addition, there is a 107 x 98 mm mass arising from the
descending colon from the site of the prior surgical resection invading the
left iliopsoas muscle, this mass also has multiple cystic and solid components
and involves the left mid-ureter causing hydroureteronephrosis of the left
kidney.

There is a tract in the subcutaneous tissues of the left side of the posterior
aspect of abdominal wall, most likely representing prior drainage of the left
psoas mass.  There is a rim-calcified focus within the second part of the
duodenum, likely a duodenal diverticulum.

The liver, CBD, right kidney, and adrenal glands appear unremarkable.  The
tail of the pancreas is abutting the mass at the splenic hilum.  The body,
head, and uncinate process of the pancreas appear unremarkable.  There is no
free fluid in the abdomen. The splenic vein is occluded and there are multiple
collaterals in the abdomen.

CT PELVIS POST ADMINISTRATION OF INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST:

There are multiple enlarged enhancing bilateral inguinal lymph nodes, the
largest of these is a 20 x 14 mm left inguinal lymph node.  The mass involving
the left iliopsoas muscle extends almost down to the level of the insertion
along the iliac crest.

There is no plane of cleavage between the descending colon and this mass in
the iliopsoas muscle suggestive of local invasion. There is no free fluid in
the pelvis.

MUSCULOSKELETAL:

The left iliopsoas mass abuts the left iliac blade, but there is no evidence
of bone invasion.  There are no definite lesions worrisome for skeletal
metastasis.

CONCLUSION:

Ovarian cancer, restaging examination.

1.  Large upper abdominal mass with its epicenter at the splenic hilum with
invasion/involvement of the spleen, splenic vasculature, pancreatic tail.

2.  Large complex mass involving the left iliopsoas muscle with
involvement/invasion of the left colon and sigmoid.

3.  Left hydroureteronephrosis due to involvement of the left ureter in the
left iliopsoas mass.

4.  Enlarged bilateral inguinal lymph nodes as described above.

5.  Non-obstructing right renal calculi.

6.  Scattered 2-mm pulmonary nodules and post-irradiation changes in the left
lung anteriorly.

radiology_facts.report_text:
INDICATION:  ___ female with ovarian cancer.  Evaluate response to
therapy.

COMPARISON:  ___.

TECHNIQUE:  Contrast-enhanced MDCT-acquired axial images of the chest, abdomen
and pelvis from the thoracic inlet to the pubic symphysis.  Three-minute
delayed images through the level of the kidneys were obtained and multiplanar
reformatted images were created.

CT OF THE CHEST WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST:  Several sub-4-mm pulmonary nodules
are again identified, unchanged from ___ left upper lobe (2:22),
left lower lobe (2:24) and lingula (2:31). Minimal biapical scarring and
nodular pleural thickening anterior to the lingula is stable.  There is no
pleural effusion or central lymphadenopathy.  The heart size is normal.  The
airways are patent to the subsegmental level.

CT OF THE ABDOMEN WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST:  The liver, gallbladder, and
adrenal glands are normal.  The kidneys enhance symmetrically.  Moderate left-
sided hydronephrosis is unchanged.  5-mm non-obstructing stone in the right
renal collecting system is unchanged.

Large heterogeneous mass with solid and cystic components centered within the
splenic hilum measuring 10.4 x 8.0 cm (2:51) is largely unchanged from prior
exam.  Loss of fat plane between the mass and the adjacent stomach wall/
pancreatic tail is most compatible with tumor invasion.  The second site of
involvement in the left retroperitoneum involving the iliopsoas with possible
tumoral invasion of the adjacent descending colon is also not significantly
changed from prior exam.  Again seen is gas within the mass which is likely
related to the enterocutaneous fistula leading to the left back (2:67).

The intra-abdominal loops of large and small bowel maintain a normal caliber.
Note is made of a 2.4-cm duodenal diverticulum in its second portion (2:65).
There is no intra-abdominal free fluid or lymphadenopathy.  The abdominal
aorta maintains a normal caliber.  The celiac trunk, SMA and ___ are patent.
Diffuse atherosclerotic calcification is seen involving the aorta and iliac
arteries.

CT OF THE PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST:  The rectum, sigmoid colon,
bladder, and pelvic loops of small bowel are within normal limits. There is no
free fluid.  A 2.2 x 0.6-cm left external iliac chain lymph node (2:95) is
minimally smaller compared to prior exam.

BONE WINDOWS:  There is no suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesion.

IMPRESSION:

1.  Compared to prior exam from ___, the large mass centered in the
splenic hilum and left retroperitoneum is not significantly changed.  Again
seen is gas within the left retroperitoneal mass, which likely relates to the
enterocutaneous fistula.

2.  Stable moderate left hydronephrosis.

3.  5-mm non-obstructing stone within the right renal collecting system.

sided hydronephrosis is unchanged.  5-mm non-obstructing stone in the right
renal collecting system is unchanged.

Large heterogeneous mass with solid and cystic components centered within the
splenic hilum measuring 10.4 x 8.0 cm (2:51) is largely unchanged from prior
exam.  Loss of fat plane between the mass and the adjacent stomach wall/
pancreatic tail is most compatible with tumor invasion.  The second site of
involvement in the left retroperitoneum involving the iliopsoas with possible
tumoral invasion of the adjacent descending colon is also not significantly
changed from prior exam.  Again seen is gas within the mass which is likely
related to the enterocutaneous fistula leading to the left back (2:67).

The intra-abdominal loops of large and small bowel maintain a normal caliber.
Note is made of a 2.4-cm duodenal diverticulum in its second portion (2:65).
There is no intra-abdominal free fluid or lymphadenopathy.  The abdominal
aorta maintains a normal caliber.  The celiac trunk, SMA and ___ are patent.
Diffuse atherosclerotic calcification is seen involving the aorta and iliac
arteries.

CT OF THE PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST:  The rectum, sigmoid colon,
bladder, and pelvic loops of small bowel are within normal limits. There is no
free fluid.  A 2.2 x 0.6-cm left external iliac chain lymph node (2:95) is
minimally smaller compared to prior exam.

BONE WINDOWS:  There is no suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesion.
sections.impression:
1.  Compared to prior exam from ___, the large mass centered in the
splenic hilum and left retroperitoneum is not significantly changed.  Again
seen is gas within the left retroperitoneal mass, which likely relates to the
enterocutaneous fistula.

2.  Stable moderate left hydronephrosis.

3.  5-mm non-obstructing stone within the right renal collecting system.

radiology_facts.report_text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN AND PELVIS

INDICATION:  Recurrent ovarian cancer.  Assess for change in tumors status.

TECHNIQUE:  CT chest, abdomen and pelvis was performed on a multidetector CT
scanner following administration of oral and intravenous contrast. 
Multiplanar reformations were carried out.

COMPARISON:  CT torso ___.

FINDINGS:

CT CHEST:

There is no evidence of mediastinal lymphadenopathy.  There is no focal lung
lesion.  There is no evidence of pericardial or pleural effusion.  No axillary
lymphadenopathy is seen.

CT ABDOMEN:

There is a large mass which tracks along the left psoas muscle and extends
posteriorly through the posterior abdominal wall to the skin.  The mass also
extends to the sigmoid and left colon.  There is a fistulous communication
between the mass and the sigmoid colon (series 2, image 91) and also to the
descending colon at the level of a surgical anastomosis (series 2, image 79
and 80).  In the ___ the mass, there is a tract of lower attenuation
material and gas, compatible with fistulous communication between the colon
and the skin through the large mass.  The mass has increased in size from
prior study.  It currently measures 8.7 cm transverse x 6.2 cm anterior to
posterior x approximately 19 cm in craniocaudal dimension.  The mass is also
inseparable from the left common and external iliac vessels.  There is
encasement of the left mid-ureter.  There is dilation of the proximal left
ureter and left collecting system with hydroureter and hydronephrosis which
are of moderate severity and worst than on prior study.

Abutting the left retroperitoneal mass, there are enlarged nodular soft tissue
lesions compatible with lymphadenopathy (series 2, image 76 and series 2,
image 78).  These have increased in size from prior study.

There is a large mass in the left upper quadrant centered at the splenic
hilum.  The mass is heterogeneous in attenuation likely indicating the
presence of some necrosis within the ___ the lesion.  It measures 9.2 x
7.8 cm in axial ___ and is similar in ___ to prior study.  The
mass extends from the splenic hilum to the posterior wall of the gastric
fundus from which it is inseparable and also extends to the tail of the
pancreas.  The splenic vein is also invaded by the mass and there is evidence
of varices along the greater curvature of the stomach (series 2, image 50).

The adrenal glands are normal in appearance.  There is no focal left renal
lesion.  There is no right renal lesion.

The liver is normal in appearance with no evidence of focal lesion or biliary
dilatation.  The portal vein is patent.

CT PELVIS:

The mass in the left flank as described above extends into the pelvis.  There
is left external iliac chain lymphadenopathy with nodes measuring up to 1.5 x
2.9 cm in diameter, these have enlarged from prior study.  No right-sided
pelvic lymphadenopathy is seen.  There is no ascites.

No aggressive focal bone lesion is seen.  There are mildly enlarged left
inguinal lymph nodes measuring up to 1 cm in short axis diameter.  These have
slightly increased from prior study.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Large mass which extends along the left psoas muscle and tracks out to the
skin. This has a fistulous communication at two separate points with colon,
involving the sigmoid colon and descending colon.  This mass has increased in
size from prior study.

2.  Similar-appearing large mass in the left upper quadrant involving the
spleen and extending to the stomach and pancreas.

3.  Left hydronephrosis and hydroureter is of moderate severity and has
worsened compared to prior study.

4.  Increase in left common iliac chain lymphadenopathy.

radiology_facts.report_text:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  ___ female with ovarian cancer, on chemotherapy. 
Assess disease status.

TECHNIQUE:  Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, pelvis was performed
following the administration of both oral along with 100 cc of intravenous
Optiray.  Multiplanar reformatted images were also obtained.

COMPARISON STUDY:  CT abdomen and pelvis from ___.  CT torso
from ___.

FINDINGS:

CONTRAST-ENHANCED CT OF THE CHEST:

The lungs are unremarkable, without evidence of pulmonary nodule or focal
consolidation.  The tracheobronchial tree is patent to the subsegmental
levels.  There are coronary arterial calcifications.  The heart size is
normal.  The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber.  Scattered enlarged right
axillary lymph nodes are identified, the largest measuring 1.9 cm x 1.5 cm
(image 15 of series 4).  A prominent 1.1 cm left axillary node is also
identified.  A necrotic-appearing lymph node is identified in the left
superior mediastinum, which measures 1.8 cm x 1.6 cm (image 15 of series 4). 
There is no abnormally enlarged hilar lymphadenopathy.

CONTRAST-ENHANCED CT OF THE ABDOMEN:

The liver, gallbladder, right adrenal gland, and right kidney are
unremarkable.  There is no intra- or extra-hepatic biliary duct dilation.  A
large mass is again seen arising from the gastric fundus with extension to and
invasion of the splenic hilum and splenic vessels.  Nodularity of the adjacent
omentum is also present.  Scattered wedge-shaped hypodensities are noted in
the splenic periphery, which were noted previously and likely representing
infarcts.  Overall appearance of this mass is not significantly changed from
the previous exam.  There is a moderate degree of left-sided hydronephrosis
and proximal hydroureter, essentially unchanged.  As described on the previous
study, there is a large soft tissue mass which arises from the left
retroperitoneum and demonstrates anterior displacement of the left kidney. 
The mass appears to involve the entire left psoas muscle and tracks outwards
through the flank, into the left flank subcutaneous tissues and ultimately the
overlying skin.  Fluid and feculent material is noted within this mass, which
was noted on prior study.  The overall size and appearance of this mass has
decreased relative to the previous exam, with both decrease in the amount of
internal feculent material along with the overall size of the collection. 
There is also a low density collection seen within the left psoas, which has
decreased in size. As on the prior study, the mass tracks inferiorly along the
left hemipelvis.  The descending colon is not identified in isolation of this
mass and thus appears to be involved.

Again noted is a diverting ileostomy in the right lower quadrant.  There are
no dilated loops of bowel identified.  Multiple areas of necrotic-appearing
retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy are essentially stable.  Numerous large
anterior pelvic wall and inguinal lymph nodes are again noted, essentially
unchanged in size and appearance.  Specifically, a lateral right inguinal node
was previously measured at 3.6 cm x 2.0 cm, and currently measures
approximately 3.7 cm x 1.9 cm.  The bladder is unremarkable in appearance.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:

No suspicious lytic or blastic lesions are identified.

IMPRESSION:

1.  Interval decrease in size of large left retroperitoneal mass with fistula
to the overlying left flank skin and subcutaneous tissues.  The associated
hypodense collection within the left psoas muscle, located within this mass,
has also decreased, and currently measures 2.1 cm x 2.4 cm.  Associated
left-sided hydronephrosis and proximal hydroureter is unchanged.
2.  Essentially stable appearance of left upper quadrant mass, which appears
to be extending from the gastric fundus to the splenic hilum.
3.  Multiple hypodense peripheral changes in the spleen, likely representing
stable splenic infarcts.
4.  Unchanged retroperitoneal and inguinal lymphadenopathy.
5.  Bilateral axillary and superior mediastinal lymphadenopathy, which are new
compared with the prior CT of the torso from ___ (more recent CTs
of the abdomen and pelvis did not include the chest).

radiology_facts.report_text:
HISTORY:  ___ female with chest x-ray findings suggestive of possible
PE, flu from ten and one-half months ago.  Rule out pulmonary embolism.

COMPARISON:  Chest PA and lateral ___.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT axial images were obtained from the thoracic inlet is upper
abdomen with administration of IV contrast.  Coronal, sagittal and oblique
reformatted images were obtained.

CT OF THE CHEST WITH IV CONTRAST:  The heart and pericardium are normal,
without pericardial effusion.  The great vessels are within normal limits,
without evidence for thoracic aortic dissection or pulmonary embolism.

The lungs are clear without consolidation or pulmonary edema.  There is a 3-mm
pulmonary nodule within the right lower lobe (2:75).  There is no pleural
effusion.  No mediastinal, hilar or axillary lymphadenopathy is appreciated.
Limited views of the upper abdomen are unremarkable.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:  No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesion is identified.

IMPRESSION:
1.  No acute process, without evidence of thoracic aortic dissection or
pulmonary embolism.

2.  3-mm pulmonary nodule within the right lower lobe.  If there are no risk
factors for intrathoracic malignancy, no further followup for this is
necessary.

Findings posted to the ED dashboard at the time of interpretation.

radiology_facts.report_text:
INDICATION:  ___ female with diffuse bilateral infiltrates on outside
hospital chest x-ray and hemoptysis.  Assess for pulmonary masses or other
cause for hemoptysis.

COMPARISON:  Chest radiograph from ___ and from ___.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT axial images of the chest were obtained with intravenous
contrast.  Coronal, sagittal and axial MIP reformations were prepared.

CT CHEST WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST:  The thyroid gland is homogeneous without
focal nodule.  No supraclavicular or axillary adenopathy is identified.  There
are bulky mediastinal lymph nodes; a representative precarinal node measures
up to 13 mm in short axis diameter.  Subcarinal adenopathy measuring up to 16
mm (2:32) is noted.  There are bulky bilateral hilar lymph nodes as well.  The
thoracic aorta is non-aneurysmal and demonstrates no signs of acute aortic
syndrome.  Central pulmonary arteries are patent.  The heart size is normal,
and there is no pericardial effusion.

The tracheobronchial tree is patent to subsegmental levels without bronchial
wall thickening or bronchiectasis.  There are multiple areas of consolidation
throughout both lungs, most severe in the left upper lobe, lingula, left lower
lobe, and right lower lobe.  The right upper and middle lobes are relatively
less involved.  The consolidation has mixed solid and ground-glass attenuation
and follows a peribronchovascular distribution.  Areas of septal thickening
are also evident.  There is no pleural effusion.  Findings most likely reflect
multifocal pneumonia with reactive adenopathy, however, diffuse alveolar
hemorrhage is possible in the appropriate clinical setting.  A focal 11 x 9 mm
nodule is identified arising from the medial aspect of the minor fissure
(2:36).  There is no pleural effusion.  

Limited views of the upper abdominal viscera appear within normal limits.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Diffuse solid and ground-glass consolidations predominantly involving the
left upper and lower lobes and right lower lobe with peribronchovascular
distribution.  Findings are concerning for multifocal pneumonia with
associated reactive adenopathy, though diffuse alveolar hemorrhage is possible
in the appropriate clinical setting.  Neoplastic process is also not excluded.

2.  11 mm parafissural pulmonary nodule of uncertain etiology.  


Would recommend followup CT examination after acute symptoms have resolved (3
months or sooner) to ensure resolution of opacities and focal nodule.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CT CHEST W/O CONTRAST

INDICATION:  ___ with Microscopic Polyangitis, presenting with small volume
hemoptysis, evaluate for diffuse alveolar hemorrhage.

TECHNIQUE:  Multidetector helical scanning of the chest was performed without
intravenous contrast agent reconstructed as contiguous 5- and 1.25-mm thick
axial, 2.5-mm thick coronal and parasagittal, and 8 x 8 mm MIPs axial images.

DOSE:  Total DLP (Body) = 237 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  Chest radiographs dated ___.

FINDINGS: 

The thyroid is normal. Supraclavicular, axillary, mediastinal and hilar lymph
nodes are not enlarged. Aorta and pulmonary arteries are normal size. Cardiac
configuration is normal and there is no appreciable coronary calcification. 
There is a trace pericardial effusion.

Diffuse scattered areas of ground-glass opacity in a peribronchovascular
distribution could represent inflammatory disorder, an infectious process, or
diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, depending on the clinical setting.  This process
is more confluent in the medial aspect of the right upper and middle lobes
(601b:  46, 35).  More focal nodular areas in bilateral lungs (02:17, 21, 28,
30, 49, 55), are likely more confluent areas of the same process.  There is
associated diffuse cylindrical bronchiectasis.  Short interval followup chest
CT after resolution of the acute process is recommended to exclude underlying
malignancy.  The airways are patent to the subsegmental level.

There is moderate dextroscoliosis of the thoracic spine.  There is no
worrisome osseous lesion.  The partially visualized upper abdomen is
unremarkable.  There is a small hiatal hernia.

IMPRESSION:


1. Diffuse areas of ground-glass opacity in a peribronchovascular distribution
could represent an inflammatory disorder, an infectious process, or diffuse
alveolar hemorrhage, depending on the clinical setting.
2. Several more nodular opacities are seen bilaterally which may represent
extension of the underlying process described above, however short interval
follow-up chest CT after symptoms have resolved is recommended to ensure
resolution.

___ with Microscopic Polyangitis, presenting with small volume
hemoptysis, evaluate for diffuse alveolar hemorrhage.
sections.technique:
Multidetector helical scanning of the chest was performed without
intravenous contrast agent reconstructed as contiguous 5- and 1.25-mm thick
axial, 2.5-mm thick coronal and parasagittal, and 8 x 8 mm MIPs axial images.

DOSE:  Total DLP (Body) = 237 mGy-cm.
sections.findings:
The thyroid is normal. Supraclavicular, axillary, mediastinal and hilar lymph
nodes are not enlarged. Aorta and pulmonary arteries are normal size. Cardiac
configuration is normal and there is no appreciable coronary calcification. 
There is a trace pericardial effusion.

Diffuse scattered areas of ground-glass opacity in a peribronchovascular
distribution could represent inflammatory disorder, an infectious process, or
diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, depending on the clinical setting.  This process
is more confluent in the medial aspect of the right upper and middle lobes
(601b:  46, 35).  More focal nodular areas in bilateral lungs (02:17, 21, 28,
30, 49, 55), are likely more confluent areas of the same process.  There is
associated diffuse cylindrical bronchiectasis.  Short interval followup chest
CT after resolution of the acute process is recommended to exclude underlying
malignancy.  The airways are patent to the subsegmental level.

There is moderate dextroscoliosis of the thoracic spine.  There is no
worrisome osseous lesion.  The partially visualized upper abdomen is
unremarkable.  There is a small hiatal hernia.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CTA chest

INDICATION:  ___ year old woman with hypoxia, tachypnea, tachycardia.// Please
eval for PE, eval for bronchiectasis per CTA H/N recs.

TECHNIQUE:  Axial multidetector CT images were obtained through the thorax
after the uneventful administration of intravenous contrast.
Reformatted coronal, sagittal, thin slice axial images, and oblique maximal
intensity projection images were submitted to PACS and reviewed.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Spiral Acquisition 3.0 s, 39.7 cm; CTDIvol = 4.5 mGy (Body) DLP = 180.1
mGy-cm.
   2) Stationary Acquisition 0.6 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 1.5 mGy (Body) DLP = 0.8
mGy-cm.
   3) Stationary Acquisition 2.4 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 6.2 mGy (Body) DLP = 3.1
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 184 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  CTA head and neck dated ___, CT chest dated ___.

FINDINGS: 

The aorta and its major branch vessels are patent, with no evidence of
stenosis, occlusion, dissection, or aneurysmal formation.  There is no
evidence of penetrating atherosclerotic ulcer or aortic arch atheroma present.

The pulmonary arteries are well opacified to the subsegmental level, with no
evidence of filling defect within the main, right, left, lobar, segmental or
subsegmental pulmonary arteries.  The main and right pulmonary arteries are
normal in caliber, and there is no evidence of right heart strain.  The heart
is not enlarged.  There is a small pericardial effusion.

There is no supraclavicular, axillary, or hilar lymphadenopathy.  Multiple
prominent although not technically enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes are
present, likely reactive.  The thyroid gland appears unremarkable.

There is no pneumothorax or pleural effusion.

Airways are patent centrally.  Centrilobular and paraseptal emphysematous
changes are noted bilaterally.  Biapical pleuroparenchymal scarring is present
there is diffuse cylindrical bronchiectasis and bronchial wall thickening,
which has progressed as compared to ___, and is most notable in the bilateral
upper lobes.  Extensive ___, nodular and ground-glass opacities are
seen throughout all pulmonary lobes, new from ___. The largest pulmonary
nodule is located in the right upper lobe, and measures 11 mm in mean diameter
(301:64).  Subpleural reticulation is seen at the bilateral lung bases. 
Mucous plugging is noted within small airways.  Focal atelectasis versus
scarring is seen involving a portion of the right lower lobe (602:29).

Limited images of the upper abdomen are notable for a small hiatal hernia. 
Incidental note is made of a small accessory spleen.  There is diffuse
thickening of the left adrenal gland, without focal nodule identified.

No lytic or blastic osseous lesion suspicious for malignancy is identified. 
Again noted is moderate dextroscoliosis of the thoracic spine.

IMPRESSION:


1. No evidence of pulmonary embolism or acute aortic abnormality.
2. Nonspecific diffuse multifocal ___, nodular, and ground glass
opacities favor an infectious or inflammatory process.  The ground-glass
component is nonspecific, though could be partially explained by mild diffuse
alveolar hemorrhage, given the patient's history.
3. Diffuse cylindrical bronchiectasis and bronchial wall thickening has
progressed since ___.
4. Small pericardial effusion.
5. Pulmonary nodules, measuring up to 11 mm in mean diameter.
 For incidentally detected multiple solid pulmonary nodules bigger than 8mm, a
CT follow-up in 3 to 6 months is recommended in a low-risk patient, with an
optional CT follow-up in 18 to 24 months. In a high-risk patient, both a CT
follow-up in 3 to 6 months and in 18 to 24 months is recommended.

See the ___ ___ Guidelines for the Management of Pulmonary
Nodules Incidentally Detected on CT" for comments and reference:
___

RECOMMENDATION(S):  Pulmonary nodules, measuring up to 11 mm in mean diameter.
For incidentally detected multiple solid pulmonary nodules bigger than 8mm, a
CT follow-up in 3 to 6 months is recommended in a low-risk patient, with an
optional CT follow-up in 18 to 24 months. In a high-risk patient, both a CT
follow-up in 3 to 6 months and in 18 to 24 months is recommended.

radiology_facts.report_text:
INDICATIONS:  ___ man with resected gastric glomus tumor.  Question
recurrence.

COMPARISONS:  ___.

TECHNIQUE:  Axial non-contrast CT images of the abdomen were obtained followed
by CT imaging of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with oral and intravenous
contrast.  Delayed-phase imaging through the level of the kidneys only was
also performed.  Sagittal and coronal reformats were obtained.

CT OF THE CHEST WITH IV CONTRAST:  A nodule along the lower pole of the left
thyroid lobe measures 31 x 25 mm in axial ___, not significantly
changed.  There is no axillary, hilar, or mediastinal lymphadenopathy.
Coronary artery calcifications are noted.  The heart, great vessels, and
pericardium are otherwise unremarkable.  There are no pleural or pericardial
effusions.  The lungs are clear, without nodules.

The liver, gallbladder, pancreas, spleen, adrenal glands, and kidneys appear
normal.  The patient is status post partial gastrectomy.  There is no evidence
of recurrent tumor in the stomach including near the suture line.  Post-
surgical appearance of the stomach, small and large bowel, is unremarkable.
There are stable small retroperitoneal lymph nodes.

CT OF THE PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST:  The prostate, seminal vesicles, and
bladder appear normal.  A few sigmoid diverticula are noted.  A subcentimeter
right pelvic side wall lymph node is unchanged, and not enlarged by CT size
criteria.  There is no free fluid.

BONE WINDOWS:  There are no suspicious lytic or blastic lesions.  A few small
sclerotic foci in the pelvis are unchanged and most likely to represent bone
islands.

IMPRESSION:  No evidence of recurrent or metastatic disease.

radiology_facts.report_text:
STUDY TYPE:  CT TORSO

COMPARISON:  CT torso from ___.

INDICATION:  ___ man with resected gastric glomus tumor.  Please
evaluate for history of recurrent disease.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT-acquired contiguous axial slice of 5 mm slice thickness
acquired from the top of the lungs to pubic symphysis.  Coronal and sagittal
reconstructions were obtained.

FINDINGS:
CT OF THE CHEST WITH IV CONTRAST:  Multiple metastatic lung nodules are
evident in bilateral lungs, which were not present on previous studies.  The
largest lesion on the right side is in the right lower lobe, located
subpleurally and measuring about 7.8 mm x 7.8 mm.  The largest lesion on the
left side is present in the left upper lobe and measures about 7.4 mm x 10 mm.
No pleural effusion.  The heart and the great vessels appear normal. Scattered
___ axillary and mediastinal lymphadenopathy is noted.

CT OF THE ABDOMEN WITH IV CONTRAST:  Innumerable hepatic hypodense lesions
with peripheral rim enhancement suggestive of metastatic involvement are seen.
The largest lesion is seen in the right posterior lobe and measures about 5.3
cm.  Accurate evaluation of the liver is severely limited because of gantry
contact artifact.  However, there is clear evidence of innumerable metastatic
hepatic lesions.  Multiple pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are noted in
the porta hepatis and gastrohepatic ligament region.  The spleen, pancrease,
kidneys, adrenals, ureters, intraabdominal bowel loops appear normal.  There
is evidence of soft tissue density in the anterior abdominal wall, which could
be a delayed rectus sheath hematoma or could be metastatic deposit.

CT OF THE PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST:  The rectum, sigmoid colon, prostate,
seminal vesicles, and urinary bladder appear unremarkable.  There is evidence
of pathologically significant lymphadenopathy along the right iliac chain and
in the right inguinal region.  There is pelvic peritoneal metastatic nodule
measuring about 2.7 cm x 3.3 cm.

Osseous windows: there is no evidence of suspicious lytic or sclerotic osseous
lesions.

Coronal and sagittal reconstructions delineated the above defined pathology.

IMPRESSION:
1. Interval development of widespread metastatic involvement.

2. Bilateral pulmonary nodules, multiple scattered hypodense hepatic nodules,
scattered mediastinal, mesenteric and pelvic lymphadenopathy, consistent with
metastasis.

3. Solitary peritoneal metastatic nodule is noted in the pelvis.

4. Anterior abdominal wall soft tissue mass, which could be a delayed rectus
sheath hematoma, (post laparascopy) or due to metastatic disease

The above results were communicated to Dr. ___.

radiology_facts.report_text:
INDICATION:  ___ man with resected gastric glomus tumor, now with new
mass.  Assess status of disease.

___.

TECHNIQUE:  Multidetector helical scanning of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis
was performed following the administration of oral and 130 cc IV Optiray
contrast.  Non-contrast and delayed images were obtained through the upper
abdomen.  Coronal and sagittal reformats were displayed.

CT OF THE CHEST:  Again seen is prominence of the left lobe of the thyroid
gland measuring 2.5 x 2.3 cm.  There are no pathologically enlarged
mediastinal, hilar, or axillary lymph nodes.  The heart, pericardium, and
great vessels are normal.  Several lung nodules are identified including a
stable 6-mm nodule in the left lower lobe (3:20), a slightly increased 7-mm
nodule in the right middle lobe (3:28, previously 4 mm), and a stable 7-mm
nodule in the right lower lobe (3:40).  No new nodules are identified.

Again seen are innumerable hepatic metastases with rim enhancement, which are
more conspicious and slightly increased in size compared to the prior exam.
The gallbladder, spleen, adrenal glands, kidneys, and pancreas are normal. The
patient is status post partial gastrectomy with a suspicious soft tissue mass
at the anastomosis measuring 5.2 x 4.9 cm (previously 5 x 3 cm).  Two
additional nodules anterior to the gastric antrum have also increased in
size (3:78).  A mildly enhancing soft tissue mass is seen within the left
anterior rectus muscles measuring 6.6 x 5.6 cm (increased compared to 4.7 x
3.9 cm), with an additional satelite nodule more superficially which is not
entirely included on today's exam. There are several additional peritoneal
implants including a new 2.1 x 1.1 cm nodule lateral to the right edge of the
liver (3:75) and drop metastasis in the right deep pelvis measuring 4.7 x 2.3
cm (3:25), increased from 3.2 x 2.7 cm. The intra- abdominal small and large
bowel loops are normal.

CT OF THE PELVIS:  The sigmoid colon, rectum, and bladder are normal.  There
is no pelvic free fluid or lymphadenopathy.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:  No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Increased metastatic disease burden including increased soft tissue masses
at the gastric anastomosis and left anterior abdomen.  Also increased size of
peritoneal and drop metastases.
2.  Innumerable hepatic metastases, which are more conspicuous and slightly
larger than prior exam.
3.  Pulmonary nodules, most of which are stable; however, one nodule has
increased in the right middle lobe.

flat_lung_rads_output.uncertainty_note: density_category 缺失或不明确，按 solid 路径保守处理。 缺少 smoking_eligibility，不阻断规则，但限制风险分层解释。

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CT CHEST W/CONTRAST

INDICATION:  Colonoscopy demonstrating a circumferential sigmoid mass.
Evaluate for metastatic disease.

TECHNIQUE:  Multidetector helical scanning of the chest was coordinated with
intravenous infusion of nonionic iodinated contrast agent reconstructed as
contiguous 5- and 1.25-mm thick axial, 2.5-mm thick coronal and parasagittal,
and 8 x 8 mm MIPs axial images. Acquisitions were part of a chest, abdomen and
pelvis for which abdomen and pelvis images were separated to the evaluated by
the abdominal imaging service.

DOSE:  DLP: Reported separately on the abdomen and pelvis dictation.

COMPARISON:  CT abdomen and pelvis ___. Chest radiograph ___.

FINDINGS: 

Heterogeneous right thyroid lobe demonstrates no focal nodularity.

Though the study is not designed for cardiac chamber evaluation, it shows that
the heart is mildly enlarged. There is no significant pericardial fluid.
Aortic annular and coronary artery calcifications are mild. Thoracic aortic
arch and main pulmonary artery are normal caliber. There is no central
pulmonary embolus.  Several scattered mediastinal lymph nodes measure up to 8
mm in short axis in the left paratracheal station (06:22), but are not
pathologically enlarged. There is no pathologic supraclavicular, axillary,
hilar or mediastinal lymphadenopathy.

Airways are patent to the subsegmental level. Moderate to severe paraseptal
emphysema is apical predominant, less prominent in the lung bases. Lingular,
right lower lobe and right middle lobe linear atelectasis is mild.  Subpleural
nodule in the posterior segment of the right upper lobe measures 3 mm (8:130).
Lungs are clear without focal consolidation. Pleural surfaces are clear
without effusion or pneumothorax.

Bones and soft tissues: 3 mm well-circumscribed sclerotic focus in the
anterior portion of the T4 vertebral body likely represents a bone island.
Thoracic cage is intact without suspicious focal lesion. Thoracic degenerative
changes are mild.

IMPRESSION:


1. No evidence of intrathoracic metastasis.
2. 3 mm right upper lobe nodule lacks suspicious features though requires
followup examination in ___ year in this patient with a pre-existing malignancy.
3. Moderate to severe apical predominant paraseptal emphysema is less
prominent at the bases.
4. For abdomen and pelvis findings including abnormal gallbladder, please
refer to the separate dictation under clip ___.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CT CHEST W/CONTRAST

INDICATION:  ___ man with resected colon cancer and ___ status post
RFA.

TECHNIQUE:  Axial contrast enhanced CT images through the chest. Coronal,
sagittal and thin-section reformats were generated.  The study is technically
limited due to inadvertent expiratory phase and respiratory motion.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence: 1) Spiral Acquisition 3.1 s, 34.1 cm; CTDIvol =
2.3 mGy (Body) DLP = 76.6 mGy-cm. 2) Sequenced Acquisition 0.5 s, 0.2 cm;
CTDIvol = 9.0 mGy (Body) DLP = 1.8 mGy-cm. 3) Stationary Acquisition 6.2 s,
0.2 cm; CTDIvol = 101.2 mGy (Body) DLP = 20.2 mGy-cm. 4) Spiral Acquisition
4.5 s, 28.9 cm; CTDIvol = 8.7 mGy (Body) DLP = 246.5 mGy-cm. 5) Spiral
Acquisition 10.6 s, 69.1 cm; CTDIvol = 8.5 mGy (Body) DLP = 581.9 mGy-cm. 6)
Spiral Acquisition 5.0 s, 32.5 cm; CTDIvol = 8.7 mGy (Body) DLP = 276.6
mGy-cm. Total DLP (Body) = 1,204 mGy-cm. ** Note: This radiation dose report
was copied from CLIP ___ (CT ABD AND PELVIS W AND W/O CONTRAST, ADDL
SECTIONS)

COMPARISON:  ___ dating back to ___

FINDINGS: 

FINDINGS:

NECK, THORACIC INLET, AXILLAE, CHEST WALL: An 8 mm hypodense nodule in the
right lobe of the thyroid and a 7 mm left nodule are unchanged.  No axillary
lymphadenopathy.

UPPER ABDOMEN: For details regarding the abdomen and pelvis including multiple
hypodensities in liver please see dedicated abdomen and pelvis CT report.

MEDIASTINUM AND HILA: Borderline mediastinal and right hilar lymph nodes
appears slightly larger than prior for example a left paratracheal node
measures 0.9 x 1.9 cm, previously 0.7 x 1.8 cm (___:31).  A periaortic node
measures 0.9 x 1.5 cm, previously 0.9 x 1.4 cm (08:31).  A right hilar node
measures 1.1 x 1.4 cm previously 1.3 x 1.3 cm (08:42).  The subtle differences
in measurement could be due to intervertebral expiratory phase.

HEART and PERICARDIUM: Cardiac configuration is normal and there is moderate
coronary calcification.
PLEURA: No pleural effusion.
LUNG:

1. PARENCHYMA: Paraseptal and centrilobular emphysema remains severe. 
Evaluation of the pulmonary parenchyma is limited by respiratory motion and in
overt and expiratory phase which precludes assessment of the known nodule and
limits assessment for new nodules.
2. AIRWAYS: Thickening of the airways in the lower lobes appears new with some
areas of mucous plugging in the left lower lobe (9:176).
3. VESSELS: Although this study was not tailored for the evaluation of the
pulmonary arteries, no filling defect is identified in the central pulmonary
arteries.
CHEST CAGE: The sclerotic focus in the T4 vertebral body is unchanged dating
back to ___.

IMPRESSION:



1. Technically limited study due to respiratory motion and inadvertent
expiratory phase of respiration. This precludes assessment of known pulmonary
nodule and limits assessment for new pulmonary nodules.  If clinically
indicated, the patient could return at no additional charge for repeat imaging
with additional breathing instructions.
2. Borderline mediastinal and right hilar lymph nodes appear slightly larger
than prior;  however the subtle differences in measurement could potentially
be due to differences in respiratory phases.
3. For details regarding the abdomen pelvis please see dedicated abdomen
pelvis CT.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CT CHEST W/CONTRAST

INDICATION:  ___ man with ___ and colon cancer in remission.

TECHNIQUE:   Multi-detector helical scanning of the chest was coordinated with
intravenous infusion of nonionic, iodinated contrast agent, reconstructed as
contiguous 5 mm and 1.0 or 1.25 mm thick axial, 2.5 or 5 mm thick coronal and
parasagittal, and 8 mm MIP axial images. Sequential scanning of the abdomen
and pelvis  will be reported separately. Images of the chest were reviewed.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence: 1) Spiral Acquisition 3.0 s, 33.6 cm; CTDIvol =
2.1 mGy (Body) DLP = 69.4 mGy-cm. 2) Sequenced Acquisition 0.5 s, 0.2 cm;
CTDIvol = 7.1 mGy (Body) DLP = 1.4 mGy-cm. 3) Stationary Acquisition 6.1 s,
0.2 cm; CTDIvol = 79.0 mGy (Body) DLP = 15.8 mGy-cm. 4) Spiral Acquisition 2.8
s, 18.4 cm; CTDIvol = 8.1 mGy (Body) DLP = 143.3 mGy-cm. 5) Spiral Acquisition
10.5 s, 68.2 cm; CTDIvol = 7.8 mGy (Body) DLP = 528.8 mGy-cm. 6) Spiral
Acquisition 2.8 s, 18.4 cm; CTDIvol = 8.1 mGy (Body) DLP = 143.3 mGy-cm. Total
DLP (Body) = 902 mGy-cm. ** Note: This radiation dose report was copied from
CLIP ___ (CT ABD AND PELVIS W AND W/O CONTRAST, ADDL SECTIONS)

COMPARISON:  ___

FINDINGS: 

FINDINGS:

NECK, THORACIC INLET, AXILLAE, CHEST WALL: 12 mm hypodense lesion in the right
lobe of thyroid (8, 7).  No supraclavicular or axillary adenopathy.  No gross
breast lesions. Mild gynecomastia.

UPPER ABDOMEN: Will be reported separately.

MEDIASTINUM: Mediastinal lymph nodes (lower paratracheal, AP window and
subcarinal) appear similar compared to prior.

HILA: No new or enlarging hilar lymph nodes. Partially calcified right hilar
lymph nodes (9, 110).

HEART and PERICARDIUM: Heart size is top normal. No pericardial effusion.  No
aortic valve calcification. Mild LAD calcification.  Mild right coronary
artery calcification.  Mild atherosclerotic changes of the thoracic aorta.
PLEURA: No pleural effusion.
LUNG:

-PARENCHYMA: Moderate centrilobular and paraseptal emphysematous changes
predominantly in the upper lobes.  Mild, but diffuse bronchial wall
thickening. Motion artifact and suboptimal inspiratory phase obscures the
lower lung zones for small pulmonary nodules and fine interstitial changes. 
Linear and posterior basal subpleural micro atelectasis.
-AIRWAYS: The airways are patent to the subsegmental level.
-VESSELS: Pulmonary artery measures within normal limits.  No filling defects.
CHEST CAGE: Spondylotic changes of the thoracic spine. No lytic/ destructive
bony lesions.  Sclerotic lesion in T4 is unchanged.

IMPRESSION: 

Motion artifact and suboptimal inspiratory phase reduce sensitivity for
dectecting small pu lmonary nodules and fine interstitial changes, but taking
this into account there is no new or enlarging pulmonary nodules or masses.

The mediastinal lymph nodes are essentially unchanged.

For abdominal findings please refer to CT abdomen report.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CTA CHEST WITH CONTRAST

INDICATION:  History: ___ with +ddimer, chest pain  // ? PE

TECHNIQUE:  Axial multidetector CT images were obtained through the thorax
after the uneventful administration of 100 cc of Omnipaque intravenous
contrast.  Reformatted coronal, sagittal, thin slice axial images, and oblique
maximal intensity projection images were submitted to PACS and reviewed.

DOSE:
Acquisition sequence:
   1) Stationary Acquisition 3.0 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 9.1 mGy (Body) DLP = 4.6
mGy-cm.
   2) Spiral Acquisition 3.7 s, 28.6 cm; CTDIvol = 9.5 mGy (Body) DLP = 271.8
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 276 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  CT abdomen and pelvis on ___ and CT chest on ___

FINDINGS: 

THYROID,  LYMPH NODES & MEDIASTINUM: The visualized thyroid appears within
normal limits.  Mediastinal lymph nodes are unchanged from the prior
examination in ___.  There is no hilar, axillary or supraclavicular
lymphadenopathy.  Calcified right hilar lymph nodes are re- demonstrated.

HEART & VESSELS: There is no pulmonary embolism to the segmental level. 
Subsegmental branches are not well evaluated due to respiratory motion.  The
great vessels are normal in caliber.  There is no evidence of aortic injury. 
The heart is normal in size and there is no pericardial abnormality.

LUNGS & AIRWAYS: Moderate centrilobular and paraseptal emphysematous changes
predominate within the upper lobes as before.  Mild, diffuse bronchial wall
thickening is re- demonstrated.  Breathing motion artifact limits evaluation
for small pulmonary nodules.  There is minimal atelectasis at the lung bases. 
No pneumothorax or focal consolidation.

UPPER ABDOMEN: Limited views of the upper abdomen shows a nodular liver
consistent with cirrhosis.  Numerous hypodensities within the liver are better
characterized on recent CT from ___.  There is a simple cyst in the
upper pole of the right kidney.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES & SOFT TISSUES: Sclerotic lesion in T4 is stable.  No
suspicious osseous lesions.  Note is made of a chronic anterior rib fracture
of the ___ rib.


IMPRESSION: 

Subsegmental pulmonary arterial branches are not well evaluated due to
respiratory motion, particularly in the lower lobes.  No evidence of pulmonary
embolism seen elsewhere.  No acute aortic dissection.

Moderate centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema is overall stable from the
recent prior exam.  Bibasilar atelectasis without focal consolidation or
pleural effusion.

Mediastinal lymph nodes are grossly stable.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CTA CHEST WITH CONTRAST

INDICATION:  History: ___ w/ HCC (active) and colon cancer (remission) who
presents w/ chest discomfort, dizziness, cough. Evaluate for PE or aortic
abnormality

TECHNIQUE:  Axial multidetector CT images were obtained through the thorax
after the uneventful administration of intravenous contrast. Reformatted
coronal, sagittal, thin slice axial images, and oblique maximal intensity
projection images were submitted to PACS and reviewed.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence: 1) Stationary Acquisition 2.5 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol
= 7.6 mGy (Body) DLP = 3.8 mGy-cm. 2) Spiral Acquisition 3.6 s, 27.9 cm;
CTDIvol = 8.6 mGy (Body) DLP = 240.5 mGy-cm. Total DLP (Body) = 244 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  ___

FINDINGS: 

HEART AND VASCULATURE: Pulmonary vasculature is well opacified to the
subsegmental level without filling defect to indicate a pulmonary embolus. The
thoracic aorta is normal in caliber without evidence of dissection or
intramural hematoma.  Trace atherosclerotic calcification of the aorta.  The
right pulmonary artery is mildly enlarged, measuring 2.6 cm.  The heart,
pericardium, and great vessels are otherwise within normal limits. No
pericardial effusion is seen.
 AXILLA, HILA, AND MEDIASTINUM:  Unchanged calcified right hilar lymph nodes. 
Interval increase in size of an AP window lymph node, measuring 1.5 x 1.5 cm
(___), previously 1.4 x 1.3 cm.  Mediastinal lymphadenopathy is otherwise
unchanged. For example, a left lower paratracheal lymph node measures 1.7 x
0.9 cm (___).  No axillary, mediastinal, or hilar lymphadenopathy is present.
No mediastinal mass. PLEURAL SPACES: No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

LUNGS/AIRWAYS:  Respiratory motion limits evaluation fine pulmonary nodules. 
No large pulmonary nodules or masses.  Unchanged moderate upper lobe
predominant centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema.  Bibasilar atelectasis. 
Unchanged mild, but diffuse bronchial wall thickening, consistent with chronic
airways disease. The airways are patent to the level of the segmental bronchi
bilaterally.

BASE OF NECK:  Unchanged subcentimeter hypodensity in the right hemi thyroid,
of indeterminate clinical significance.  Visualized portions of the base of
the neck show no abnormality.

ABDOMEN: Included portion of the upper abdomen again demonstrate a cirrhotic
liver with numerous subcentimeter hypodensities and a large lesion in hepatic
segment VII, consistent with known hepatocellular carcinoma.  Mild
splenomegaly, measuring 13.3 cm.

BONES: No suspicious osseous abnormality is seen.? There is no acute fracture.
Unchanged sclerotic focus in the T4 vertebral body, likely representing a bone
island.  Healed right anterior third rib fracture.

IMPRESSION:


1. No evidence of pulmonary embolism or aortic abnormality.
2. Unchanged moderate upper lobe predominant centrilobular and paraseptal
emphysema as well as mild, diffuse bronchial wall thickening, consistent with
chronic airways disease.
3. Extensive mediastinal lymphadenopathy with at least 1 lymph node larger the
prior study.
4. Cirrhotic liver with numerous subcentimeter hypodensities and a large
lesion in hepatic segment VII, consistent with known hepatocellular carcinoma.

sections.findings:
HEART AND VASCULATURE: Pulmonary vasculature is well opacified to the
subsegmental level without filling defect to indicate a pulmonary embolus. The
thoracic aorta is normal in caliber without evidence of dissection or
intramural hematoma.  Trace atherosclerotic calcification of the aorta.  The
right pulmonary artery is mildly enlarged, measuring 2.6 cm.  The heart,
pericardium, and great vessels are otherwise within normal limits. No
pericardial effusion is seen.
 AXILLA, HILA, AND MEDIASTINUM:  Unchanged calcified right hilar lymph nodes. 
Interval increase in size of an AP window lymph node, measuring 1.5 x 1.5 cm
(___), previously 1.4 x 1.3 cm.  Mediastinal lymphadenopathy is otherwise
unchanged. For example, a left lower paratracheal lymph node measures 1.7 x
0.9 cm (___).  No axillary, mediastinal, or hilar lymphadenopathy is present.
No mediastinal mass. PLEURAL SPACES: No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

LUNGS/AIRWAYS:  Respiratory motion limits evaluation fine pulmonary nodules. 
No large pulmonary nodules or masses.  Unchanged moderate upper lobe
predominant centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema.  Bibasilar atelectasis. 
Unchanged mild, but diffuse bronchial wall thickening, consistent with chronic
airways disease. The airways are patent to the level of the segmental bronchi
bilaterally.

BASE OF NECK:  Unchanged subcentimeter hypodensity in the right hemi thyroid,
of indeterminate clinical significance.  Visualized portions of the base of
the neck show no abnormality.

ABDOMEN: Included portion of the upper abdomen again demonstrate a cirrhotic
liver with numerous subcentimeter hypodensities and a large lesion in hepatic
segment VII, consistent with known hepatocellular carcinoma.  Mild
splenomegaly, measuring 13.3 cm.

BONES: No suspicious osseous abnormality is seen.? There is no acute fracture.
Unchanged sclerotic focus in the T4 vertebral body, likely representing a bone
island.  Healed right anterior third rib fracture.
sections.impression:
1. No evidence of pulmonary embolism or aortic abnormality.
2. Unchanged moderate upper lobe predominant centrilobular and paraseptal
emphysema as well as mild, diffuse bronchial wall thickening, consistent with
chronic airways disease.
3. Extensive mediastinal lymphadenopathy with at least 1 lymph node larger the
prior study.
4. Cirrhotic liver with numerous subcentimeter hypodensities and a large
lesion in hepatic segment VII, consistent with known hepatocellular carcinoma.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CT CHEST W/CONTRAST

INDICATION:  ___ year old man with  colon cancer and ___// eval for recurrent
colon cancer and HCC.

TECHNIQUE:  Multidetector helical scanning of the chest was coordinated with
intravenous infusion of nonionic iodinated contrast agent and reconstructed as
contiguous 5 mm and 1.25 mm thick axial, 2.5 mm thick coronal and
parasagittal, and 8 mm MIP axial images.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Spiral Acquisition 4.1 s, 32.6 cm; CTDIvol = 5.3 mGy (Body) DLP = 172.4
mGy-cm.
   2) Stationary Acquisition 4.2 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 20.2 mGy (Body) DLP =
10.1 mGy-cm.
   3) Spiral Acquisition 3.8 s, 24.8 cm; CTDIvol = 19.7 mGy (Body) DLP = 487.7
mGy-cm.
   4) Spiral Acquisition 8.2 s, 64.8 cm; CTDIvol = 19.0 mGy (Body) DLP =
1,230.0 mGy-cm.
   5) Spiral Acquisition 3.1 s, 24.6 cm; CTDIvol = 18.8 mGy (Body) DLP = 464.1
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 2,364 mGy-cm.
** Note: This radiation dose report was copied from CLIP ___ (CT ABD AND
PELVIS WITH CONTRAST)

COMPARISON:   CTA chest ___.

FINDINGS: 

NECK, THORACIC INLET, AXILLAE:  The imaged thyroid has a 1.0 cm hypodense
nodule in the left lobe and a 1.3 cm hypodense nodule in the right lobe. 
Multiple bilateral axillary lymph nodes are increased in size since the prior
study.

MEDIASTINUM:  Mediastinal lymph nodes are not enlarged.

HILA:  Hilar lymph nodes are not enlarged.

HEART:  The heart is not enlarged and there is moderate coronary arterial
calcification.  There is no pericardial effusion.

VESSELS: Aortic arch configuration is conventional.  Aortic caliber is normal.
The main, right, and left pulmonary arteries are normal caliber.  Filling
defects are seen in the right lower lobe subsegmental pulmonary arterial
branches (6:110) compatible with pulmonary emboli, new since the prior study.

PULMONARY PARENCHYMA: Severe centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema is again
noted.  Numerous pulmonary nodules are scattered lungs measuring up to 1.8 cm
in the left upper lobe (6:69), all new since the prior chest CT.

AIRWAYS:  The airways are patent to the subsegmental level bilaterally.

PLEURA:  There is no pleural effusion.

CHEST WALL AND BONES:  An 8 mm sclerotic focus in the left posterior second
rib and a 9 mm sclerotic focus along the anterior aspect of the eighth
vertebral body are unchanged since the prior study.  A chronic right anterior
seventh rib fractures noted.  Chronic T12 compression fracture is again noted.
Multilevel degenerative changes are moderate.

UPPER ABDOMEN:  Please see separately submitted Abdomen and Pelvis CT report
for subdiaphragmatic findings.

IMPRESSION:


1. Right lower lobe subsegmental pulmonary arterial filling defects compatible
with recent pulmonary emboli, new since the prior study.
2. Numerous bilateral lung nodules, new since, are likely metastases.
3. Bilateral hypodense nodules in the thyroid measure up to 1.3 cm on the
right.  Further evaluation with thyroid ultrasound is recommended if
clinically appropriate.
4. Please see separately submitted Abdomen and Pelvis CT report for
subdiaphragmatic findings.


RECOMMENDATION(S):  Thyroid US.

NOTIFICATION:   The findings were discussed with ___, M.D. by
___, M.D. on the telephone on ___ at 4:54 pm, 10 minutes
after discovery of the findings.

radiology_facts.report_text:
INDICATION:  ___ year old man with new metastatic lesions in
liver/coon/thyroid/lung. Oncology most concerned for lung lesions, please
biopsy.// Lung mets, need tissue diagnosis to guide therapy

COMPARISON:  ___ CT abdomen and pelvis

PROCEDURE:  CT-guided right lung biopsy.

OPERATORS:  Dr. ___, radiology trainee and Dr. ___
radiologist. Dr. ___ supervised the trainee during the key
components of the procedure and reviewed and agrees with the trainee's
findings.

TECHNIQUE:  The risks, benefits, and alternatives of the procedure were
explained to the patient.  After a detailed discussion, informed written
consent was obtained.  A pre-procedure timeout using three patient identifiers
was performed per ___ protocol.

The patient was placed in a right lateral position on the CT scan table. 
Limited preprocedure CTscan of the intended biopsy area was performed.  Based
on the CT findings an appropriate position for the biopsy was chosen.  The
site was marked.

The site was prepped and draped in the usual sterile fashion.  1% lidocaine
were administered to the subcutaneous and deep tissues for local anesthetic
effect.  Under CT guidance, a 17 gauge coaxial needle was introduced into the
lesion.  An 18 gauge core biopsy device with a 11 mm throw was used to obtain
two core biopsy specimens, which were sent for pathology.

The procedure was tolerated well and there were no immediate post-procedural
complications.

DOSE:  Total DLP (Body) = 479 mGy-cm.

SEDATION:  Moderate sedation was provided by administering divided doses of 0
mg Versed and 50 mcg fentanyl throughout the total intra-service time of 18
minutes during which patient's hemodynamic parameters were continuously
monitored by an independent trained radiology nurse.

FINDINGS: 

Multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules, as before.

IMPRESSION: 

Successful CT-guided core biopsy of right upper lobe nodule with moderate
intraparenchymal hematoma surrounding the biopsied lesion.

radiology_facts.report_text:
PROCEDURE:  CT chest with contrast on ___.

COMPARISON:  CT torso ___.

TECHNIQUE:  After the administration of 75 cc of IV Optiray, contiguous axial
images were obtained from the thoracic inlet to the subdiaphragmatic area.
Thinner slice 5-mm and 1.25-mm images were reconstructed in the axial plane at
different window algorithm.  Sagittal/coronal reformatted images were also
obtained for evaluation.

HISTORY:  ___ man with metastatic melanoma, evaluate disease status
and include bi-dimensional measurements for all lesions and create an oncology
table.

FINDINGS:  There are multiple mediastinal lymph nodes.  The lymph nodes
located in the aortopulmonary window and in the prevascular location are all
less than 1 cm in size and not changed since the prior examination.  The
largest of the mediastinal lymph nodes located in the right hilar and
subcarinal area has substantially increased in size on today's examination.
The right subcarinal lymph node measures 4 x 5.5 cm while the right hilar
lymph node measures 2.7 x 3 cm.  The subcarinal lymph node is designated as
target lesion #1.  The right infrahilar soft tissue mass measures 4.8 x 3.9 cm
and is slightly enlarged from the previous examination(target#2
); however, the post- obstructive bronchiectasis that were filled with mucoid
impaction has partially resolved.
On today's examination, the superior segment of the right lower lobe is re-
aerated with slight reexpansion of the right lower lung.  No lung lesion
suspicious for metastasis is noted in either lung.  No pleural or pericardial
effusion is noted.
The heart size is within normal limits.
The bony structures do not show any lesion suspicious for malignancy and/or
infection. The limited evaluation of the abdomen on this contrast-enhanced
examination demonstrate multiple hypodense lesions with probable enhancing rim
seen in the liver, the largest lesion is located in the right posterior
portion of the liver.
There is also increase in size of the bilateral adrenal nodules; the right
adrenal nodule measures 1.1 x 1.2 cm while the left adrenal nodule measures
2.9 x 3 cm.

The right thyroid nodule is smaller in size on today's examination.

IMPRESSION:

1.  Interval progression of metastatic disease in the chest and lung
characterized by increase in size of the right hilar, subcarinal lymph node
and right infrahilar soft tissue mass; however, with a less post-obstructive
mucoid impaction.

2.  New liver lesions and increase in size of the bilateral adrenal metastatic
lesions.


The new oncology table has been placed online.

radiology_facts.report_text:
CT TORSO

COMPARISON:  None.

HISTORY:  Metastatic melanoma.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT axial acquired images of the chest, abdomen and pelvis were
obtained.  130 cc of Optiray IV contrast was administered.  Coronal and
sagittal reformats were performed.

FINDINGS:  
CT CHEST:  There is a 2.0 x 1.8 cm right thyroid gland nodule.  Scattered
subcentimeter prevascular and pretracheal lymph nodes are identified.  A large
subcarinal lymph node measuring 3.1 x 4.3 cm (103, 32) is designated as target
lesion  #1.  Multiple right hilar lymph nodes (103, 30) measure up to 2.5 cm x
1.7 cm.  A large lower right hilar soft tissue mass (103, 39) measures
approximately 3.5 cm x 3.6 cm is designated as target lesion #2, and appears
to obstruct the right lower lobe basal trunk bronchus. Subsequently, extensive
mucus impaction is identified in the right lower lobe basilar segments.  The
soft tissue mass appears to cause mass effect and narrowing of the right
middle lobe bronchus.  Secretions are seen within the distal trachea, proximal
left main stem bronchus and right main bronchus. There is no left hilar
adenopathy.  The left main stem bronchus and its branches are patent to the
subsegmental level.  Areas of emphysematous changes are identified primarily
in the right upper lobe.  There are no other lung masses or nodules
identified.  There is no pericardial or pleural effusion identified.
Calcifications of the left anterior descending artery are noted. The great
vessels are unremarkable.

CT OF THE ABDOMEN:  The spleen, pancreas, kidneys, liver, and gallbladder are
unremarkable.  A 2.1 x 2.0 cm lesion is identified in the tail of the left
adrenal gland, which enhances upon contrast administration and is designated
as target lesion  #3. This lesion is concerning for adrenal metastases.  The
right adrenal gland is normal.  Within a focal segment of jejunal bowel loops
(103, 88) there is a large filling defect concerning for a jejunal mass.
Adjacent to surgical sutures seen within the small bowel, there is a small
cystic-appearing lesion (103, 96), which likely represents a peritoneal
inclusion cyst secondary to post-surgical changes.  There is no free fluid or
free air.  There is no mesenteric or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy
identified.

CT OF THE PELVIS:  The prostate gland, bladder, sigmoid colon and rectum are
unremarkable.  There is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy identified.  

BONE WINDOWS:  An old healed right inferior pubic ramus fracture is
identified.  There are no suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions identified.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Right lower hilar soft tissue mass obstructing the right lower lobe
bronchus trunk to basilar segments causing extensive mucus impaction.  
2.  Subcarinal and right hilar pathologically enlarged lymph nodes.
3.  Right thyroid nodule.  Ultrasound is recommended for further evaluation.
4.  Left adrenal nodule measuring 2.1 x 2.0 cm, concerning for metastases.  
5.  Focal small bowel filling defect concerning for small bowel mass as
described above.

An oncology table was created under Care Web with bidimensional measurements.

radiology_facts.report_text:
REASON FOR EXAM:  Shortness of breath.  Clinical concern for pulmonary
embolism.

COMPARISON:  Multiple chest radiographs with the most recent from ___.

TECHNIQUE:  Multidetector CT images of the chest before and after
administration of IV contrast as per routine pulmonary embolism protocol were
obtained.

FINDINGS:  This study is limited due to timing of contrast bolus.  A few
apparent filling defects in the left upper lobe and right lower lobe
subsegmental branches likely represents mixing artifact related to timing of
contrast bolus or streak artifact.  No definite filling defects are identified
to suggest pulmonary embolism. The aorta appears unremarkable.  The heart is
not enlarged.  There is no pericardial effusion.  There are a few
subcentimeter mediastinal and hilar lymph nodes with no evidence of
lymphadenopathy by size criteria.   Soft tissue density in the right lower
lobe adjacent to the bifurcation of right lower lobe subsegmental bronchi may
represent a lymph node versus less likely a pulmonary nodule measuring up to
0.6 cm, (3:73).  There is patchy ground-glass opacity in the lingula, and
smaller focus of ground glass opacity in bilateral lower consistent with
inflammatory-infectious etiology.   There is a 0.6 cm nodular density in the
right lower lobe (3:98) with adjacent linear opacity, as well as a second
adjacent nodule measuring up to 0.5 cm, (2:48). Nodule vs. focal atelectasis
in the right middle lobe measures 4 mm. The visualized tracheobronchial tree
is grossly patent.  The visualized upper abdominal organs including the
partially visualized spleen, and adrenals are grossly unremarkable.  A focal
hypodensity in the left lobe of the liver is too small to characterize. The
thyroid gland is unremarkable.

IMPRESSION:

1.  Limited study due to timing of the contrast bolus. No definite evidence of
pulmonary embolus, and no central pulmonary embolus.  In case of continued
strong clinical concern for pulmonary embolism, a repeat study can be
obtained.

2.  Patchy areas of ground-glass opacity in the lingula and bilateral lower
lobes consistent with infectious, including atypical pneumonia or aspiration,
or inflammatory etiology.  Clinical correlation recommended. 

3.  Subcentimeter nodules in right lower and middle lobes measuring up to 6
mm.   A followup chest CT is recommended to assess stability in approximately
12 months if this patient is low risk for lung cancer and 6 months if patient
is high risk for lung cancer.
