radiology_facts.report_text:
CT OF TORSO

HISTORY:  Prostate cancer with rising PSA.

TECHNIQUE:  After administration of high-density oral contrast, multidetector
axial images were carried out through the torso from the thoracic inlet down
to the pelvic outlet with intravenous administration of nonionic contrast.
Coronal and sagittal reformatted images were filmed.

COMPARISON:  Comparison is made to prior torso CTs from ___ and a chest CT from ___ as well as selected images from all
the studies.

FINDINGS:

CHEST:  There is new or progressed osseous sclerotic metastatic disease in the
thoracic bony cage including osteoblastic metastasis to the posterior right
fifth rib and to a mid thoracic left rib posteromedially (series 3, image 39).
In addition, the left fifth or sixth rib has fractured in the interim since
the ___ study (series 3, image 43) and the subacute fracture is healing
without bony ___.

In addition, the edge of osteoblastic metastatic disease at the inferior left
humeral head is evident on the first image.

No enlarged hilar or mediastinal lymph nodes are seen.  There is dense
calcified atherosclerotic plaque involving the arch of the aorta, great
vessels and coronary arteries.  A small calcified node in the left hilum
appears less apparent than on previous studies.

Evaluation of lung parenchyma shows post-surgical changes in the right upper
lobe posteriorly.  Mild emphysemastous change is seen diffusely in the upper
lobes. No suspicious new nodules are seen though a tiny, possibly calcified
nodule at the left lung base and noncalcified 2mm nodule near the left major
fissure are unchanged over more than ___ years.  A 4-mm subcutaneous nodule
posteriorly in the right chest wall is also retrospectively unchanged compared
to previous studies.

ABDOMEN:  The liver, spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands and gallbladder show no
abnormality.  A 7-8 mm simple cyst is seen at the posterior upper pole of the
left kidney, unchanged.  Both kidneys excrete contrast symmetrically, however,
the cortices are thin and there is irregularity to the contour of the kidneys
consistent with chronic renal disease.  Atherosclerotic plaque is seen in the
abdominal aorta, particularly infrarenally and there is calcified plaque
posteriorly at the origins of both renal arteries.  There is no visible
stenosis is seen (study not with technique for CTA).  The calcified left renal
stone seen on prior studies is no longer evident and is not seen along the
left ureter.

No retroperitoneal adenopathy or ascites is evident.

PELVIS:  At the time of scanning, oral contrast has reached the rectum and no
obstruction is evident.  There is soft tissue prominence of the ileocecal
valve, which appears mass-like, but is not changed compared to older studies.
Diverticulosis of the colon is noted, particularly in the region of the
sigmoid but no inflammatory change is seen.  Calcified atherosclerotic plaque
extends from the lower aorta into both iliac systems and is prominent in the
common femoral arteries and distally.

Postoperative changes are seen in the subcutaneous tissue anterior to the
bladder and there are some small surgical clips seen as well.  There is some
linear calcification at the inferior and anterior bladder wall on the right,
slowly progressed.  There is slight irregularity of bladder wall, but no mass
is seen.  The prostate is normal in size.  No pelvic sidewall or pathologic
inguinal adenopathy is seen.  Note is made of some image degradation from beam
hardening artifact from the patient's metallic right femoral hip prosthesis.
The inguinal rings are somewhat patulous bilaterally.

New osseous sclerotic bone lesions are seen including a 2.7 mm L2 vertebral
body lesion posteriorly abutting the posterior cortex of that vertebral body.
This had measured 7 mm in ___.  There is no expansion of the
vertebral body posteriorly or evidence of imminent fracture.  At the anterior
aspect of the L3 vertebral body, a tiny osseous sclerotic lesion is seen, and
there is also slightly expansile osteosclerosis of the left ischium and
another small 6-mm focus in the left posterior acetabulum.

CONCLUSION:
1.  Progression of osteosclerotic metastatic disease to the bony skeleton
including lesions of posterior ribs, left humeral head, L2 and L3 vertebral
bodies and left inferior pelvis (ischium and posterior acetabulum).  In
addition to these bony findings, the patient has fractured a left anterior rib
which is ununited, but nondisplaced.
2.  Stable postoperative changes in the lungs.  Mild stable emphysematous
changes in upper lobes.
3.  Postoperative changes on the anterior inferior bladder which includes
calcification in the wall.
4.  Bilaterally thin renal cortices progressed slowly over time.
5.  Atherosclerosis including coronary artery plaque.

sections.findings:
CHEST:  There is new or progressed osseous sclerotic metastatic disease in the
thoracic bony cage including osteoblastic metastasis to the posterior right
fifth rib and to a mid thoracic left rib posteromedially (series 3, image 39).
In addition, the left fifth or sixth rib has fractured in the interim since
the ___ study (series 3, image 43) and the subacute fracture is healing
without bony ___.

In addition, the edge of osteoblastic metastatic disease at the inferior left
humeral head is evident on the first image.

No enlarged hilar or mediastinal lymph nodes are seen.  There is dense
calcified atherosclerotic plaque involving the arch of the aorta, great
vessels and coronary arteries.  A small calcified node in the left hilum
appears less apparent than on previous studies.

Evaluation of lung parenchyma shows post-surgical changes in the right upper
lobe posteriorly.  Mild emphysemastous change is seen diffusely in the upper
lobes. No suspicious new nodules are seen though a tiny, possibly calcified
nodule at the left lung base and noncalcified 2mm nodule near the left major
fissure are unchanged over more than ___ years.  A 4-mm subcutaneous nodule
posteriorly in the right chest wall is also retrospectively unchanged compared
to previous studies.

ABDOMEN:  The liver, spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands and gallbladder show no
abnormality.  A 7-8 mm simple cyst is seen at the posterior upper pole of the
left kidney, unchanged.  Both kidneys excrete contrast symmetrically, however,
the cortices are thin and there is irregularity to the contour of the kidneys
consistent with chronic renal disease.  Atherosclerotic plaque is seen in the
abdominal aorta, particularly infrarenally and there is calcified plaque
posteriorly at the origins of both renal arteries.  There is no visible
stenosis is seen (study not with technique for CTA).  The calcified left renal
stone seen on prior studies is no longer evident and is not seen along the
left ureter.

No retroperitoneal adenopathy or ascites is evident.

PELVIS:  At the time of scanning, oral contrast has reached the rectum and no
obstruction is evident.  There is soft tissue prominence of the ileocecal
valve, which appears mass-like, but is not changed compared to older studies.
Diverticulosis of the colon is noted, particularly in the region of the
sigmoid but no inflammatory change is seen.  Calcified atherosclerotic plaque
extends from the lower aorta into both iliac systems and is prominent in the
common femoral arteries and distally.

Postoperative changes are seen in the subcutaneous tissue anterior to the
bladder and there are some small surgical clips seen as well.  There is some
linear calcification at the inferior and anterior bladder wall on the right,
slowly progressed.  There is slight irregularity of bladder wall, but no mass
is seen.  The prostate is normal in size.  No pelvic sidewall or pathologic
inguinal adenopathy is seen.  Note is made of some image degradation from beam
hardening artifact from the patient's metallic right femoral hip prosthesis.
The inguinal rings are somewhat patulous bilaterally.

New osseous sclerotic bone lesions are seen including a 2.7 mm L2 vertebral
body lesion posteriorly abutting the posterior cortex of that vertebral body.
This had measured 7 mm in ___.  There is no expansion of the
vertebral body posteriorly or evidence of imminent fracture.  At the anterior
aspect of the L3 vertebral body, a tiny osseous sclerotic lesion is seen, and
there is also slightly expansile osteosclerosis of the left ischium and
another small 6-mm focus in the left posterior acetabulum.

CONCLUSION:
1.  Progression of osteosclerotic metastatic disease to the bony skeleton
including lesions of posterior ribs, left humeral head, L2 and L3 vertebral
bodies and left inferior pelvis (ischium and posterior acetabulum).  In
addition to these bony findings, the patient has fractured a left anterior rib
which is ununited, but nondisplaced.
2.  Stable postoperative changes in the lungs.  Mild stable emphysematous
changes in upper lobes.
3.  Postoperative changes on the anterior inferior bladder which includes
calcification in the wall.
4.  Bilaterally thin renal cortices progressed slowly over time.
5.  Atherosclerosis including coronary artery plaque.

radiology_facts.report_text:
HISTORY:  Squamous cell cancer mass.  Question mass, operative planning.

COMPARISON:  Prior outside chest CT from ___.

TECHNIQUE:  Volumetric, multi detector CT of the chest was performed with
intravenous contrast.  Images are presented for display in axial plane in 5 mm
and 1.25 mm collimation.  A series of multiplanar reformation images are also
submitted for review.  

Total exam DLP:  797 mGy-cm

FINDINGS:

CT OF THE THORAX:  There is an ill-defined right perihilar mass measuring
approximately 4.1 x 3.4 cm (02:14; 601b:38) extending along the major fissure
and into the thoracic inlet, adjacent to the brachiocephalic vessels.  The
mass is causing effacement and narrowing of the right main stem bronchus. 
Surrounding fibrotic changes of the right lung could reflect changes secondary
to radiation therapy. There is a 2.1 x 1.8 x 2.4 cm left hilar nodular mass
(2:23; 601b:40). There is a moderate-sized right-sided pleural effusion with
associated compressive atelectasis.  There is a stable 4 mm nodule in the
right upper lobe and note is made of a new patch opacity at the right lung
base. The left lung is essentially clear.  

A tracheostomy tube is seen in adequate position.  The airways are otherwise
patent.  There is no mediastinal or axillary lymph node enlargement by CT size
criteria.  The heart, pericardium and great vessels are otherwise within
normal limits.  

Although this study is not designed for assessment of intra-abdominal
structures, the visualized solid organs and stomach are unremarkable.  

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:  No focal osseous lesion concerning for malignancy.  

IMPRESSION:

1. 4.1 cm right perihilar mass, causing effacement and narrowing of the right
mainstem bronchus and extending along the major fissure and into the thoracic
inlet, adjacent to the brachiocephalic vessels. 

2. 2.4 cm left hilar nodular mass.

3. Stable 4 mm nodule in the right upper lobe and new patchy opacity at the
right lung base.

4. Improved moderate sized right sided pleural effusion. 

Findings discussed with ___ by ___ via telephone on
___ 10:20 AM.

radiology_facts.report_text:
CT CHEST WITHOUT CONTRAST

REASON FOR EXAM:  ___ male patient with abnormal chest x-ray showing
bilateral hilar and right paratracheal adenopathy suggestive of sarcoidosis.
Please evaluate for adenopathy.

TECHNIQUE:  Chest MDCT was performed without contrast using 5-mm and 1.25-mm
axial slice thickness.  Supine expiration and prone images were also obtained.
Coronal reformations were reviewed in expiration and inspiration.

FINDINGS:  Multiple bihilar and mediastinal enlarged lymph nodes, up to 13 mm
in the right paratracheal region, 19 x 31 mm in the subcarinal region, 10 mm
in the left paratracheal region, 7 mm in the paraesophageal region and 11 mm 
in the aortopulmonary window.

Multiple lung nodules are scattered throughout the lungs, mostly in a
perilymphatic distribution.  Clustered peribronchovascular nodules are in the
right lower lobe. Lung nodules are almost all sub-4-mm wide, except one
11- mm nodule in the left upper lobe at the bifurcation of lingular segments
(104:128). Airways are patent to the subsegmental level. There is no pleural
or pericardial effusion.  Heart size is normal.  The main pulmonary artery is
up to 3.2 cm. Gynecomastia is asymmetrical, more marked on the left. The
thymus is normal for age.

Bones are normal.  Altough this study was not tailored for subdiaphragmatic
evaluation, the upper abdomen is unremarkable.  The spleen is normal.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Bihilar and mediastinal lymphadenopathy associated with multiple sub-4mm
mostly perilymphatic lung nodules, consistent with sarcoidosis.
2.  Left upper lobe peribronchovascular 11-mm lung nodule, probably related to
sarcoidosis, but should be closely followed up in three months to assess
stability.
3.  Enlarged pulmonary artery, could be due to pulmonary hypertension.
4.  Asymmetric gynecomastia, more marked on the left.

radiology_facts.report_text:
PATIENT HISTORY:  ___ male with significant smoking history, no major
alcohol history has three months of worsening dysphagia (no solids and
liquids), 12-pound weight loss, early satiety, lower abdominal discomfort with
EGD negative.  After one month of PPI, has gained 3 pounds blood symptoms
still persist.  

REASON FOR EXAM:  Lung mass, gastrointestinal mass, lymphadenopathy.

TECHNIQUE:  Multidetector helical scanning of the chest during infusion of
Omnipaque non-ionic intravenous contrast material agent.  Images were
reconstructed as contiguous 5- and 1.25-mm thick axial helical, 5-mm thick
coronal and parasagittal, and 8 x 8 mm MIPs projection.

COMPARISON:  There is no prior chest CT for comparison at the time of
dictation.

FINDINGS:  The thyroid gland is unremarkable.  There is no peripheral or
central lymphadenopathy.  Ascending aorta and main pulmonary artery are normal
measuring 24 and 23 mm (2:27).  Heart size is normal without pericardial
effusion.  There is no pleural effusion.  

For abdomen evaluation, please refer to separate report of concurrent CT
abdomen and pelvis, ___.  

The esophagus is normal.

BONES:  There are no bone lesions suspicious for malignancy or infection.

LUNGS AND AIRWAYS:  Airways are patent to the subsegmental level bilaterally. 
Mild biapical pleuroparenchymal scarring (301:17).  Paraseptal emphysema is
mild (301:32).  Two pleural-based nodule in the right lower lobe, measure 8 mm
and 10 mm respectively, also containg small calcifications(301:79,119). These
nodules are sitting on an unusual uneven costal surface, which might have
determined parenchymal inflammation and subsequent formation of the nodules. 
However without a prior CT a short-term followup in three months is
recommended

IMPRESSION:  
1. Two right lower lobe nodules are probably inflammatory, but without prior
CT a short-term followup in three months is recommended
2. There is no lymphadenopathy.  
3. Mild paraseptal emphysema.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CTA CHEST WANDW/O CANDRECONS, NON-CORONARY

INDICATION:  ___ year old man with new onset SOB and multiple risks: infection,
Mutliple myeloma and drug: Revlimid.  // R/o PE

TECHNIQUE:  Multi detector CT images were obtained through the chest in
arterial phase after administration of IV Omnipaque contrast.  Axial images
were interpreted in conjunction with coronal, sagittal, right oblique MIP and
left oblique MIP reformats.

212.60 mGy-cm

COMPARISON:  Comparison is made with CT abdomen and pelvis dated ___.

FINDINGS: 

CHEST CTA:

The thoracic aorta is normal caliber without evidence of aneurysm or
dissection.  The main, lobar, and segmental pulmonary arteries are well
opacified without filling defect.  Within a subsegmental branch of the
pulmonary arterial system within the posterior left lower lobe, there appears
to be intraluminal filling defect (6:212) which may represent a tiny pulmonary
embolism versus contrast mixing. The remainder of the great vessels have a
normal appearance.

CHEST:

The thyroid is normal. Axillary, supraclavicular, mediastinal, and hilar lymph
nodes are not pathologically enlarged.  The heart and mediastinum are normal.
The pericardium is intact without effusion.  Airways are patent to the
subsegmental levels.

Lung windows demonstrate bibasilar dependent atelectasis. Additionally, there
is a 1.2 cm left lower lobe, pleural-based nodule abutting the superior margin
of the left hemidiaphragm (6:231). There is no evidence of pleural effusion or
pneumothorax.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:  No focal lytic or sclerotic lesion concerning for
malignancy.

The esophagus is within normal limits. Although this study is not tailored for
the examination of subdiaphragmatic contents, the imaged portions of the
intra-abdominal organs are grossly unremarkable.

IMPRESSION: 

1. Tiny, subsegmental, left lower lobe pulmonary embolism. No evidence of
central, lobar, or segmental PE.

2. 1.2 cm pleural-based pulmonary nodule within the left lower lobe, not well
visualized on the prior examination.  Limited chest CT over the left lower
lobe could be performed 3 months for further evaluation.

NOTIFICATION:  Findings were entered into the radiology critical results
dashboard by Dr. ___ at 3:14pm on ___, 1 minute after interpretation.
